sentences.text: Well, I really didn't want to live anymore.
I didn't care anymore.
But I had very good friends in the camp, especially two-- two boys.
One was Anek Amsterdam.
And the other one was Jana Grawik.
We were very close together when my brother was still alive.
We were really close.
They came from city Kalwaria, not far from our city.
And we-- we really were like brothers, very close.
And they said, look, Maury, you have to go on in life.
Don't worry.
We-- we'll look after you.
And everything will be all right.
And I went to work.
Like, you know, I had to live with it.
It wasn't easy at first.
But I was all by myself.
Thanks to those two friends, that I just got on in life again.
And, you know, I went back to work and did my normal thing.
Well, I was-- we were working on a railway line.
We were building the-- an-- a train line-- train line for the Germans.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the work wasn't really so hard.
It wasn't easy.
But I was young, and it wasn't-- it wasn't so hard for me.
And we were always together.
Those three boys always kept together.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And things started to get worse in the camp.
They-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] one day, they told us that we're going to have an inspection in the camp.
We didn't know what the inspection's going to be.
And as probably you've heard now, because a lot of people heard of Goeth, which was in the film now, in
And he was a boss over my boss, over Oberscharfuehrer Mueller.
And our camp was opposite his, about half a kilometer or a kilometer away from-- well, half a kilometer away from each other.
And he gave an order that our camp has to have stripes.
All our suits, jackets, and trousers got to be painted, so that no one can run away or anything.
Things really started to get very bad in the camp.
So one Saturday-- no, one Sunday, I think it was.
We didn't go on Sunday to work.
And Oberscharfuehrer or Obersturmfuehrer Ion.
This was second hand to Goeth-- came into this camp.
And everyone-- there was an order that everybody has to go out from the barracks.
And we will be painted.
So we all stood in a queue to be painted.
All they had, ordinary paint, and we were just painted.
Ordinary clothes on, yes.
Because in those camps, you didn't have stripes, no.
My first stripes I had was in Germany, in Buchenwald.
So [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was standing in a queue.
And there was a Jewish policeman.
I wish I could remember his name.
And as the boys were painting, one boy, who was painted, got this Ion, took and hit them a few times.
And then he said to him, to a policeman, go and give him a jacket and paint the jacket afterwards.
So as a joke-- I didn't need a jacket in Krakau.
I didn't need anything.
A Jewish policeman was standing next to me.
And I just made a joke.
And I said, I wouldn't mind getting a jacket like this and get a couple of smacks, not realizing what I was letting myself into.
This Jewish policeman wanted to make himself very important.
So he went to this Ion and said, there's a boy here.
He would like a jacket from you.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And this Ion asked for the boy.
And I knew that's the end of me.
It's finished.
And as I went close, I was walking close to him, I knew that I'm going to be shot.
I knew this, because there's no other way.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
He wanted to make himself important, and he is-- you know, because he was a Jewish policeman.
And that's how they were.
That's how-- a nice person wouldn't be a policeman.
They were this kind of element, they were, you know?
That they-- they were good for those things.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
And when-- when I got closer to him, I stood up, and I said, 'guten tag, Herr?' How do you do?
He looked at me.
And then he said to the policeman, look, I want you to go to the storeroom and get the nicest jacket that exists in this storeroom.
And if there will be a nicer one, you're going to be shot in front of this boy, just like that.
I didn't know the reason why.
Then I found out why he wanted the jacket for me.
So the idea was every German, like him, had a young boy like me.
They used to call them the schuhputzer.
They used to clean shoes for them and do things around the place and so.
And he had a Jewish boy, like this, in the other camp.
So he wanted-- he must-- probably was my size or something.
And he wanted a jacket, from him, for-- for this boy.
So they turned upside down the storeroom to find a nice jacket, because he was afraid that he will lose his life.
He was, most probably, sorry what he did.
So when I got this jacket, he tried it on me on.
And I said, thank you very much.
And he says, look, I think you'd be most probably better off with a coat, because it's getting cold.
I said, jawohl, yes, sir.
And he asked to, this policeman, to wrap this jacket for him.
And he took the jacket with him.
So that-- that's how I imagine that it would have been for a boy, that he most probably had a boy like this, and he wanted a jacket for him.
But then I got the coat.
And that-- the end of the story, that was the thing.
But I nearly-- I nearly lost my life, there, because of this.
Yeah.
I was like-- I must say, though I went through so many concentration camps, but I must say, I had lots of Germans who helped me, too.
Really, I-- that's what I have to say.
[SIGHS]
So, then, after a short time, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we had Oberscharfuehrer Goeth-- that's the one who-- and Oberscharfuehrer Mueller.
He came to our-- to our camp, Oberscharfuehrer Goeth.
And he was fighting.
There were some arguments going on in the barrack of Mr.
Mueller.
And we didn't know what was it.
But the next day, an order came that we are all going to Plaszow.
And to go to Plaszow, we knew that it's not a happy thing.
Because we could see how those people were working there.
We--
Plaszow was the one that you saw in-- if you've seen the film, in that's what Plaszow was.
It was in Krakau, opposite our camp.
But I forgot to tell you, while I still was in Krakau, one of my sisters, Andzia, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] she was on Christian papers.
She was working, as well.
And someone denounced her.
And the Germans came and picked her up and sent her Plaszow.
So I was quite happy that we're going to Plaszow, because I thought to myself, well, I will see at least my sister.
On the way to Plaszow, as we were walking, they were killing people, as we were walking from us, which wasn't very far.
For no reason, they were taking out and just kill you in front of you.
If I would be, most probably, on this side, I could-- could have been killed.
Germans.
No, sorry, Ukraines.
Ukraines, they were.
And then, as we arrive to Plaszow, they put us-- they didn't have room for us, or they didn't want to have room.

They put us in a place where, underneath us, there were thousands of bodies lying, because this was the appellplatz, where, every day, when they were counting, that's the place where they were counting.
And there were bodies buried all over the place there.
Well, after two days, I was trying to find my sister.
I couldn't see her.
I was told that she's all right, but she can't see me.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And the next-- the following day, after that, we were transported again.
We went to the railway.
And they put us in, in carriages.
Well, they most probably had, a day before, pigs in them or whatever it was.
That's the smell that we had.
And they put in at least 100 or maybe 120 people, in a-- in a carriage, like this, where you could hardly breathe.
You couldn't sit down, because there wasn't enough room.
And without any food, without anything.
From there, they shifted us to a place in Poland called Skarzysko.
That was an ammunition factory.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] When we arrived there, after a day's traveling, we arrived there, when they opened the carriages for us, the screaming, raus, Juden.
And there were Jewish policemen.
And Germans were there.
Ukraines were there.
And we had to march to the barracks.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We've seen people, as we walked into this camp.
We've seen people, you couldn't call them really people, because some of them were yellow.
Why they were yellow?
Because they were all-- that was an ammunition factory.
And there was powder.
And from this powder, their skin got all yellow, their hands and everything, exactly like a canary.
And you couldn't wash this off or anything.
It has to take at least a few weeks.
And then, it would come off.
Because I-- I've seen people, after the war, that they were in this camp.
And they told me that, after a few weeks, it came off.
So this is how I know that it wasn't a permanent thing.
And people were wearing, instead of clothes, they were wearing cement bags.
And this was their clothes, and no shoes, no nothing.
I really don't remember, but I think it was winter.
I-- I do think so.
I think it was winter, wintertime.
Yes.
I think that was the end of '42.
I think so.
Oh, '42 or '43, look, I really must think of it.
No, maybe '43?
I think it would be '43.
Yes, I think '43 it was.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I could see what we were up to.
They took us into a barrack.
And in this barrack, it was already night.
And so in the morning, a Jewish policeman came in.
And he was screaming for everyone to get out.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we were-- we were still, though we were traveling one day, and we didn't have food and so, but we were healthy, very healthy, because in Krakau, we had plenty to eat.
There was no problem with food.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I was-- they were segregating people.
And I was the one who was supposed to unload and loading ammunition, which was going to the front.
It was very hard job.
And they could call you any time of the night or any time during the day.
And I had money with me, still then.
And I had good clothes on me.
And food, there was-- there wasn't really anything much in this camp to eat.
Because for 18 people, they give you a small loaf of bread.
And you can imagine, the person who was cutting the bread, how careful he was to cut this bread for 18 people.
And a bit of soup at night, which was not soup.
It was just a bit of hot water and who knows what it was inside there.
But that was our food.
So people in this camp were dying, by the hundreds, every day, I can say.
And they had boxes there.
And as soon as you were half-dead, they throw you into the box.
The boxes were around the barracks.
Wooden boxes?
Wooden boxes, yes.
And we could see the thing.
But at first, I had money with me.
So every day, I used to buy myself a piece of bread.
Not luxury, because I was afraid.
I didn't have too much then money.
I was afraid that, yeah, who knows how long I need it.
So every night, I bought myself a piece of bread, because you could buy there.
The Poles, our bosses were Poles.
And they were bringing in food for money.
So if you had money, you could still buy.
So the money-- then the money, I spent all my money.
Well, more than it usually-- more than usually it is.
But what I-- what my main meal was, really, I had a piece of garlic and a piece of bread.
And I used to rub the garlic on the bread.
And this was my main meal.
And you had to eat it straightaway, because if you wouldn't eat, someone would steal it.
And-- and you know, you couldn't blame him, because he was hungry.
So-- pardon me.
So the only-- the only way to survive was-- a bit longer is to eat.
And at least you knew that you got something.
And [SIGHS] after a while, I didn't have any more money.
So I had a good jacket.
So I changed the jacket for a bad jacket, changed the shoes for wooden shoes.
And this is still-- I-- I really had hardly anything on me.
And this is how I was going to work.
And people were dying from day to day.
It was so bad that one of my friend, Anek Amsterdam, said to me, look, Maury, we're not going to survive anyhow here.
It's no use to be here.
That we're working like slaves, and there's no food.
We're-- everything is nearly gone.
We haven't got money.
We haven't got anything.
We're going to be finished in the boxes like all of the others.
So he said, look, Maury, do you want to come with me?
We run-- we run away from the camp.
That was very hard.
There were-- there were Ukraines inside the camp.
Outside the camps, there were Mongolians, Mongolians who didn't-- when they were caught prisoners, and then the Germans took them into the army.
And they were guarding our camp in Skarzysko.
So it wasn't easy.
But he said-- I said, look, Anek, I don't think it will be so easy.
Why should I go and get a bullet now?
Maybe-- maybe some miracle will happen?
He said, you know, if you don't want to go-- and Jana didn't want to go either-- so he said, I go by myself.
He said, good bye.
And he went.
He went through the-- the wires.
Everything was all right.
And who caught him?
The Poles.
And they gived him to the Germans.
[SOBBING] And then, the next day, they called us in from work, because we always have to be present when they killed someone or they hanged someone.
So we always had to be present.
[SOBBING] So they brought the boy out, my friend.
[CRYING] His uncle, Mr.
Krumholtz, he was standing next to me.
And Jana Grawik was next to me.
And they brought him out.
And the German went and killed him.
And he could have killed them straight, so that he drops.
[SOBBING] But he was just-- he killing-- he was shooting him.
And the poor boy was still standing with a few bullets.
[SOBBING] And then you could see that-- so he went closer to him and give him in his head, and he fell down.
So [SOBBING] his uncle was watching.
He was crying, and he was watching.
Two weeks after, the uncle died.
And this is the life in this camp.
And the Germans were always asking for more people, because people were dying.
And they-- he needed people for-- for the things that we were doing for the ammunition factories.
There were women with us, as well, working in the camp.
They're mainly the ones who were yellow more than the men, because they had the job, where they were working on those things.
In same camp, yes.
In different barracks.
Yes, the same camp, different barracks.

[CRYING] And as the German was asking for the-- for more people, every month, one day they told him, you'd better look after your people, because, if you won't look after your people, you-- you know where you finish up?
You go to the front.
So he could see that he-- he's risking his life.
So things were starting to get better in the camp.
Instead of 18 people a bread, there were 8 people for a bread.
The soup was already a soup.
You could really call it, that you had a soup.
And what they did, which was the main thing for us-- you know all those people that went to the gas chambers in Belzec or Treblinka?
Their clothes came to our camp.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we didn't know, but one-- it happened so that one boy undid something, and he found money and those things.
So we realized that there could be money in those things that we are getting.
So I remember, like today, I teared off a pair of shoes and gold and Russian peoples-- Russian-- Rubles.
--money came from the-- from the boots.
You undid a belt, and there were-- there was money or gold or something in whatever you took.
Not everyone was as lucky, but their camp got extremely rich, because, in those clothes, they found everything.
Those people were all killed in the gas chambers or whatever.
And we got-- So our camp really had everything that we wanted.
We had food.
We had everything.
We could do anything.
Oh, yes.
Oh, yes.
I had plenty of money-- not money, but plenty of money.
And then what I had-- you know, when-- when you're already starting to get a bit better, things become easier.
The Poles, they-- who was my master, a Pole, at first, I was working for him.
He-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I got idea in the camp, because there were lots of people who had money left at Poles, with Poles.
So he used to travel to Krakau.
And I was very happy, because he always used to see my sister.
I didn't tell him it was my sister.
But I just always told him to go in there and to say hello.
So he did so.
And he used to bring, for those people from the camp, money and jewelry and everything that they had hidden, in Poland, with those Poles.
So-- and, yeah, the camp got very, very rich.
After a while, the Russian make the first offensive.
And we were happy when the war might be-- be finished soon.
So what did they try to do in the camp to bribe the-- no, sorry.
We-- we had a letter, from the outside camp, that they are the partisans, and they would like us to come out and be with them.
So we were very happy to be in-- you know?
We believed them.
And they bribed the Ukraines, gave them lots and lots of money and lots of jewelry.
And we were able to cut the wires, at this time, and to go through.
And it was a forest, middle of the night, and nearly everyone ran out from the camp.
What year was this?
No, I don't remember.
That was when the Russians-- when the Russians made their first offensive.
This is what I remember.
And what happened when we went into the forest?
Shootings were.
They started to shoot every one of us.
They were not the partisans.
They knew that the camp is very rich from the Poles that they were our masters.
They knew that everyone has got plenty of money.
They were from the AK, they used to call themselves.
And they were those right wing-- what do you call them?
Well, that was their name.
AK, they used to call themselves.
And they were killing people.
So what did we do?
We ran back in to the barracks.
So we knew, in the morning, most probably, they will take us.
We didn't have where to go.
There was no-- and-- and we were lucky to be able to find our way back into the camp.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] In the morning, the man, who was our master, the German from the camp, he came along.
And he said, look, I know what happened last night.
I know everything.
You're a free people now.
You can do what you want.
I am not going to fix the-- the wires.
If you want to run away, you can run away.
If you want to live, all you have to do is to dismantle all the machines and everything in the camp, and you'll be all right.
And he didn't fix the fence, didn't do anything.
And no one tried even to run away.
Because we didn't have where to go.
My friend was killed at this, Anek, when he ran away before.
We knew that it's useless outside there.
There's the AK.
And-- and there's no-- useless to run away.
So we try to do this.
So we did all the things.
And we went-- we've been there for quite a while.
And afterwards, they had enough of us.
So on a Saturday morning, they came out.
They told us to be ready tomorrow.
But what they did, they marked every person on a Saturday.
They already knew which one they disliked, which one they don't like, and which one is going to go on the transport.
They had marked everyone on a piece of paper.
[SIGHS] And when you went into them, you had to tell them the name, when you stand.
So Sunday morning, people were standing in the queue.
They called you out.
They just pointed like this.
The Ukraine took him away, on the spot, just in front of us, kill him.
All the kapos, the Jewish kapos, killed them on the spot, because they didn't trust them, you see?
So if you could do this for us, you could-- you will do it afterwards, as well.
And this is how lots and lots of people lost their lives there.
And then they took us to the railway.
And they took us to Petrikau.
Jana Grawik was with me.
Yes.
And Anek was killed.
And-- and they took us to Petrikau.
When we arrived to Petrikau, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] it was the middle of the night.
Gestapo came along, with dogs, with machine guns, to the railway.
And we could see outside, through the little thing that, they were carrying machine guns and dogs.
And we came out from the train, from the carriages.
And we walked.
We walked.
They didn't have where to take us.
On the way, there was a doctor with us, he-- who swallowed some tablets.
And the Germans went and took him, straightaway, with them.
And they-- where they took him, we don't know.
But we found-- we after, found out, that he was in a hospital.
